title: Setup and Stick Grip
subtitle: Good drumming starts with balance, not force.
goal: Set throne height, relaxed grip, and a centered stroke path so the kit feels comfortable.
warmup: Sit tall with feet planted and shoulders soft.
Bounce each stick lightly on the snare without squeezing.
lessonSteps: Check that the stick pivots instead of getting trapped.
Play single hits on snare, hi-hat, and kick with equal control.
Keep motion compact and repeatable.
practiceLoop: One minute per surface, then one minute alternating between them.
Reset posture if the shoulders creep up.
coachNotes: Loose hands create consistent rebound.
Comfort first, speed later.
checkpoint: The kit feels balanced and your hands stay relaxed while playing basic hits.

title: Single Stroke Grid
subtitle: Build the basic engine for every future groove.
goal: Develop even right-left stick motion and dynamic consistency with single strokes.
warmup: Play R-L-R-L on the snare at a slow tempo.
Match the sound of both hands closely.
lessonSteps: Move the pattern to hi-hat and then toms.
Accent every fourth note without speeding up.
Finish with one minute of soft singles and one minute of medium singles.
practiceLoop: Three rounds of one minute playing, 20 seconds rest.
Last round: count out loud the whole time.
coachNotes: Even strokes sound professional immediately.
Stay relaxed enough that rebound does the work.
checkpoint: Both hands sound matched through a full minute of singles.

title: Backbeat Foundation
subtitle: Lock in the basic beat that powers countless songs.
goal: Play a steady eighth-note hi-hat groove with snare on 2 and 4 and consistent kick placement.
warmup: Count 1-and-2-and-3-and-4-and while tapping hi-hat.
Add snare only on 2 and 4.
lessonSteps: Introduce kick on 1 and 3 first.
Keep the hi-hat volume steady even when the kick enters.
Play without fills or extra notes.
practiceLoop: Run the groove for 2 minutes without stopping.
Repeat once and focus only on stronger time feel.
coachNotes: The hi-hat is the ruler of the groove.
If the beat wobbles, simplify and rebuild.
checkpoint: Your backbeat stays centered and even for two full minutes.

title: Hi-Hat Control
subtitle: Teach the hands and foot to share the groove.
goal: Control hi-hat openness, tight chick sounds, and basic hand-foot coordination.
warmup: Close the hi-hat cleanly with the foot only.
Alternate closed eighth notes with one short open note.
lessonSteps: Place the open hi-hat on the and of 4 and close it on 1.
Keep snare backbeats strong while the foot closes the hats.
Avoid washing over the groove with too much open sound.
practiceLoop: 8 bars closed hats, 8 bars one open accent, repeat four times.
Finish with one recorded pass.
coachNotes: Small hi-hat details make grooves feel much more polished.
The open sound should be a choice, not an accident.
checkpoint: You can place one controlled open hi-hat accent and close it cleanly.

title: Kick Consistency
subtitle: Make the low end dependable.
goal: Strengthen kick timing and volume so the groove feels grounded instead of random.
warmup: Play quarter notes on the kick alone.
Match the volume of every note as closely as possible.
lessonSteps: Add hi-hat eighth notes over the kick pulse.
Place extra kick notes on simple offbeats without flamming.
Return to the basic pulse and compare the feel.
practiceLoop: One minute basic pulse, one minute simple pattern, repeat three times.
Rest if the leg tightens up.
coachNotes: Consistency beats cleverness.
Strong kick control makes every groove feel more expensive.
checkpoint: The kick lands evenly and supports the groove without rushing.

title: Ghost Note Entry
subtitle: Add detail without disturbing the pocket.
goal: Use very soft snare ghost notes between backbeats while keeping the main pulse clear.
warmup: Play loud snare on 2 and 4 only first.
Tap tiny ghost notes on the snare while whisper-counting the subdivision.
lessonSteps: Keep the contrast between ghost notes and backbeats obvious.
Add the kick and hi-hat only after the snare balance feels right.
Treat ghost notes like texture, not extra backbeats.
practiceLoop: Play an 8-bar groove with ghost notes, then 8 bars without.
Notice how the pocket changes.
coachNotes: Ghost notes should feel like seasoning.
If they distract from the groove, they are too loud or too busy.
checkpoint: Your ghost notes add motion without weakening beats 2 and 4.

title: Fill Construction
subtitle: Build fills that lead back into the groove cleanly.
goal: Create short fills that keep time and return to beat 1 without panic.
warmup: Count one full bar of groove plus one beat of fill.
Play simple 8th-note fills on snare and toms only.
lessonSteps: Keep the subdivision identical between groove and fill.
Start with one-beat fills before trying two-beat fills.
Crash back into beat 1 with intention and balance.
practiceLoop: Four bars groove, one-beat fill, repeat for 10 cycles.
Then try a second fill choice and compare which lands cleaner.
coachNotes: A simple fill that lands is better than a flashy fill that derails the band.
Always know where beat 1 is.
checkpoint: You can insert short fills without losing the pulse.

title: Shuffle Feel
subtitle: Teach the groove to lean and swing.
goal: Play a basic shuffle with triplet subdivision awareness and confident backbeat placement.
warmup: Say trip-let trip-let while tapping the hi-hat.
Play the long-short shuffle subdivision on one surface first.
lessonSteps: Add snare on 2 and 4 with a relaxed bounce.
Introduce a simple kick pattern that supports the swing feel.
Keep the shuffle skipping forward instead of dragging.
practiceLoop: Two minutes hi-hat only, then two minutes full groove.
Finish with one recorded take.
coachNotes: Shuffle comes from internal subdivision more than from the limbs.
Let the groove bounce.
checkpoint: Your shuffle groove feels swung instead of straight.

title: Linear Groove Basics
subtitle: Learn to make space inside busy patterns.
goal: Build simple linear grooves where no two limbs hit together and the flow still feels musical.
warmup: Play single strokes moving between hi-hat, snare, and kick.
Keep every note the same subdivision at first.
lessonSteps: Arrange a short pattern where each limb takes one slot at a time.
Repeat the phrase until it loops cleanly.
Add one accent to give the pattern shape.
practiceLoop: Loop one linear phrase for 3 minutes.
Then return to a basic backbeat and notice how much easier it feels.
coachNotes: Linear grooves only sound good when the spacing is confident.
Do not rush the kick notes into the next beat.
checkpoint: You can repeat one simple linear groove without collisions or timing drift.

title: Song Form Drummer
subtitle: Practice making a whole arrangement feel intentional.
goal: Use groove choices, fills, and dynamics to shape intro, verse, chorus, bridge, and outro.
warmup: Map a simple song form on paper before playing.
Choose one main groove and one bigger chorus groove.
lessonSteps: Keep the verse restrained and the chorus more open.
Use fills only to point at transitions.
End the song cleanly instead of trailing off.
practiceLoop: Play the full form twice and adjust only one section at a time.
Record the second take and check whether each section feels different.
coachNotes: The drummer tells the band how the section should feel.
Big music comes from section contrast, not nonstop activity.
checkpoint: You can shape a full song form with clear dynamic changes and transitions.
